data.title: Couples Are Spending More on Weddings Than Ever — and 84% Say Tariffs Are Making It Worse. Here's the Debt They're Taking On
data.description:
The average cost of a wedding in America has climbed to $34,000, according to the latest Real Weddings Study from The Knot.

That number is the average, but the reality is more complicated.

The wedding market has effectively split in two, mirroring the broader K-shaped economy. Couples are either spending big on a curated, multi-day experience backed by family money, or they are cutting everything they can to make the numbers work on their own.

The top quarter of spenders are putting more than $41,000 into their wedding day, hosting an average of 141 guests at full-service venues like historic buildings and country clubs. Seventy-seven percent say the guest experience is the top priority. 

Family picks up about 62% of that bill on average, which removes most of the financial pressure and lets couples layer in welcome parties, rehearsal dinners and day-after brunches without losing sleep over it.

The bottom quarter is working with $12,000 or less. Ninety-two guests on average, smaller and more personal venues, and a lot of DIY. Forty-four percent make their own décor. Sixty-seven percent have a friend or family member officiate. The focus is on the marriage, and the celebration reflects that. 

The economic backdrop is making both ends of that spectrum harder. More than 85% of couples told The Knot the economy had changed their planning. Inflation forced 55% to rework their original vision. And among the couples who did adjust their budget because of economic pressure, most ended up spending more, not less. 

Once you are committed to the day and staring down vendor quotes, cutting back is harder than stretching the budget.

Tariffs are piling on, too. Eighty-four percent of couples say weddings cost more today than they did two years ago, and 78% are worried tariffs will push costs higher still, according to Zola. For the one in three couples who said tariffs had already affected their planning, the response was largely practical. 

A third moved purchases forward to lock in prices. Others shifted toward local and American-made vendors. Sixty-two percent leaned on their wedding professionals to help navigate it.

Thirty-one percent of couples are taking on credit cards or personal loans to bridge the gap. More than half, 59%, have put off buying a home to pay for the wedding. Fifty-two percent have delayed other major life milestones.

Getting a financial plan in place early matters more than most couples realize. Pausing retirement contributions for even a few years costs far more than it appears to in the moment, thanks to compounding. 

None of that changes the fact that weddings still matter enormously to the people having them. Eighty-four percent of couples say their wedding day is the thing they are looking forward to most. Three in four say it is worth the money.

That probably will not change. But the debt that comes with it deserves just as much planning as the seating chart. 

For couples starting a marriage with a balance on their cards or a retirement account on pause, working with a financial advisor in that first year to build a real repayment and savings plan is one of the better financial decisions they can make together. 

SmartAsset makes that first conversation easy, free to start and matched to advisors who actually work with people at this stage of life.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: She Left Her Federal Job After 24 Years For A Part-Time Job At TJ Maxx. 'I No Longer Have To Take Xanax'
data.description:
Masson, who worked at the Social Security Administration for about 24 years, retired in December and took a part-time job at TJ Maxx. The pay is far lower, but she says the trade-off has been worth it.

Stress, Policy Changes And Burnout Pushed Her Out

Masson said she worked as a claims representative and bilingual translator, helping people navigate Social Security benefits. While she found meaning in the work, the environment became increasingly difficult in recent years.

Don't Miss:

Staffing shortages meant the workload didn't ease, even as fewer employees were available to handle it. At the same time, she said the removal of telework made her job significantly harder.

“Telework really prolonged my career,” she told BI, explaining that working from home during COVID allowed her to focus better and be more productive.

Once that flexibility disappeared, the pressure intensified. Long wait times frustrated the public, and that frustration often landed on employees.

“It wasn't just the stress of the job itself; it was also constantly feeling demeaned by the Trump administration as being lazy and a drain on the American system,” she said, adding that public attitudes toward federal workers had shifted as well.

Over time, the stress began affecting her health. She was already taking anti-anxiety medication and started using Xanax to cope. She also has an autoimmune condition that worsens under stress.

“The more I thought about it, the more I thought, I cannot continue like this,” she told BI.

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

A Lower-Paying Job, But A Better Life

Today, Masson works about 15 hours a week at TJ Maxx, earning $12.50 an hour. It's a major drop in income, but she says her quality of life has improved dramatically.

“I'm happy — the happiest I've been in a while,” she said.

The biggest difference is how she feels day to day. Her stress has dropped and her health has improved. “I no longer have to take Xanax,” she told BI. “My stress is pretty much gone.” 

She's also intentionally avoided taking on more responsibility at her new job.

“Nope, no thank you. I'm happy just being a worker bee,” she said. “That’s all I want to do.”

“Every day feels like a weekend,” she said. “I can get up and take my time. I’m not rushing.”

Not A Realistic Option For Everyone

Online reactions to the story show a mix of admiration and skepticism.

Some people shared similar experiences, saying leaving high-stress government roles resulted in major health improvements, including better blood pressure, weight loss, and lower stress levels.

But many pointed out that her situation isn't easily replicated.

Others questioned whether retail work would feel as appealing over time, adding that customer-facing roles come with their own challenges.

Still, Masson doesn't regret her decision.

“I haven't regretted for one single second that I retired,” she said. “I’m sad for my former coworkers, and the amount of stress they must be under with fewer people.”

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: RoboSense Crosses Into Profitability As Robotics Demand Powers Record Growth
data.description:
RoboSense Technology Co., Ltd. has reported a quarterly net profit for the first time, pointing to a turning point as its robotics-focused LiDAR shipments surged. The company said the Q4 2025 result beat what the market had been bracing for, helped by a sharp jump in volume and a richer product mix.

As per the report shared with Benzinga, RoboSense posted about RMB 104 million in net income for Q4 2025 on revenue of roughly RMB 751 million, alongside operating profit of around RMB 130 million. The release also put full-year 2025 revenue near RMB 1.94 billion and said gross margin improved to 26.5%.

RoboSense’s Historic Profit Marks Industry Shift

Management tied the profitability milestone to three changes: a move from pilot runs to scaled delivery for digital products, faster expansion in robotics, and manufacturing savings linked to internally developed chips. For 2025, the company reported gross profit of about RMB 514 million, up 81.3% from the prior year.

Mark Qiu, the company's CEO and executive director, said, "We are thrilled to announce that in the fourth quarter of 2025, RoboSense achieved its first-ever quarterly profit since inception, marking a clear inflection point in our operations." Qiu also said, "Our strategic direction is clear: RoboSense is a robotics company."

RoboSense said the robotics unit accounted for about 49% of product sales revenue in Q4, with robotics revenue around RMB 347 million. The company reported quarterly robotics and other LiDAR shipments of about 221,200 units, up 2,565.1% from a year earlier and 523.1% from the prior quarter.

Can LiDAR Demand Sustain This Growth?

For the full year, RoboSense said total LiDAR deliveries reached about 912,000 units, a 67.6% year-over-year gain. It also said it shipped roughly 303,000 units into the robotics market in 2025, claiming the top global spot for that segment.

The company's outlook called for higher 2026 shipments as more automakers and robotics customers adopt LiDAR, according to Prnewswire. RoboSense also said it is aiming for annual manufacturing capacity of four million units in 2026 to support that plan.

In robotaxi, RoboSense said its "EM4 main LiDAR + E1 blind spot LiDAR" pairing has become a common choice among autonomous driving customers. The company said its coverage of major global robotaxi and robotruck players has expanded to more than 90% in the digital era, naming Didi Autonomous Driving, Baidu's Apollo Go, WeRide, and Pony.ai.

Strategic Moves Amid LiDAR Market Challenges

Profitability Trends Transforming LiDAR Landscape

How Robotics Is Driving Revenue Explosion

RoboSense said it has logged design wins covering more than 167 vehicle models across 35 automakers, with 69 models reaching start of production. It also reported 33 overseas design wins with 14 international or joint-venture brands spanning Asia-Pacific, Europe, and North America.

On the robotics side, RoboSense said its LiDAR is used across categories including robotic lawnmowers, autonomous delivery robots, humanoid robots, embodied AI, and commercial cleaning machines. The company said digital LiDAR solutions have been taken up by more than 90% of leading unmanned delivery vehicle companies, and it reported partnerships with nearly 50 humanoid and quadruped robot companies.

RoboSense also highlighted its in-house chip approach, saying it built products on SPAD-SoC and 2D VCSEL digital chip architecture and sells models including EM4, Airy, E1R, EMX, and Fairy. It said it expanded beyond sensing into components such as its Active Camera series and dexterous hands, and reported deploying its first embodied intelligence solution by the end of 2025.

data.title: Melinda French Gates Almost Quit Microsoft At 25. Now She Has A Simple Rule for Giving Feedback Without Losing Your Cool: 'Being Clear Is Kind'
data.description:
Melinda French Gates doesn’t avoid difficult conversations. She just doesn’t rush into them either.

“I’m not going to tell you right away, because I need time to think it through,” she told Bloomberg’s Leaders with Francine Lacqua podcast earlier this month. “If I’m angry about something, [I do this] to calm down. That’s on me.”

Clarity Is Kindness

The rule, she explains, isn’t about softening criticism, but about making it land. “Being clear is kind,” French Gates said, “because I’m giving them feedback so they can actually grow and become better.”

The 48-hour window also works as a signal in the other direction: once it closes without a word, employees know they’re in the clear. “If they pass the 48-hour mark, they can be confident that the job they did was a good job. You’re not going to get to your performance review and have a surprise.”

A Style Forged At Microsoft

The philosophy has roots in her early career. French Gates spent nine years at Microsoft, joining straight out of college into what she described as “the boys’ debate society” — a hard-charging culture where she found herself mirroring an aggressive style that didn’t sit right with her.

“About two years in — so I would’ve been about 25 at the time — I realized I didn’t like myself,” she said. “I didn’t like how I was treating people outside of work, because I was treating them the same way I was treating people inside of work.” She considered quitting. Instead, she pivoted to a leadership style that was firm but also nurturing — and to her surprise, it worked.

“I actually rose in the company,” she said.

French Gates currently leads Pivotal Ventures, the investment and incubation company she founded in 2015, and directs $12.5 billion received as part of her divorce settlement toward philanthropic causes globally.

The 48-hour rule, it turns out, wasn’t just a workplace habit. It became a leadership philosophy: “I don’t mind conflict. I learned to do it in a way for me that maintained my integrity.”

Disclaimer: This content was partially produced with the help of AI tools and was reviewed and published by Benzinga editors.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Tim Draper On Bitcoin And The Future Of The Financial System: 'After The Automobile, People Still Ride Horses For A While'
data.description:
Venture capital legend Tim Draper has a one-line verdict on the future of banking, likening the evolution of the financial system to the historical shift from horses to automobiles.

Is the Horse Running Out of Road?

"After the automobile, people still ride horses for a while," he told Benzinga, addressing whether AI will transform banking or replace it entirely. Draper argues that all major innovations follow the same pattern. The old system lingers even as the new one takes hold and everyone benefits from the change.

Evidence of this can be seen in the fintech world. In November 2025, industry executives at Benzinga’s Fintech Day and Awards confirmed that most firms remain in AI’s early “assist” phase, suggesting the technology is enhancing existing systems rather than replacing them.

Draper’s Bitcoin Conviction

When asked whether Bitcoin could quadruple in under two years, Draper said he is following the model built around the halvings. ‘It is a very good bet if you chart history,’ he said.

Earlier in the interview, the vocal Bitcoin maximalist also highlighted his view that, “At some point, Bitcoin will eclipse the dollar as the dollar becomes less relevant.”

‘Commerce Almost Frictionless’

Draper envisions a future where raising capital, paying employees and suppliers and settling taxes all happen in Bitcoin via blockchain smart contracts.

“Commerce almost frictionless,” he said. “No need for accountants, auditors, bookkeepers, tax collectors.”

Draper believes regulators will come around once they see the incentive — the taxes collected in a Bitcoin-based economy, he said, would dwarf what governments collect today.

Banks vs. Bitcoin: The Clarity Act

The thought leader also criticized the Clarity Act, designed to regulate digital assets, warning that traditional banks are shaping the legislation to protect their own interests and calling the current Senate compromise "worse than no bill at all."

The Same Horse, New Rails

While institutions adopt blockchain, Draper's vision of a fully on-chain economy remains ahead of the curve.

The horse, it seems, is learning to run on new rails, but it is still the same horse, because JPMorgan’s blockchain adoption streamlines payment settlement while leaving the broader institutional apparatus — the accountants, auditors and lawyers Draper wants replaced — firmly intact.

Photo Courtesy: Tim Draper
